mnemonics.title: Euler's Theorem Formula
mnemonics.content: E-X-F: Euler says 'X partial X + Y partial Y equals N times F' → Remember: X·Px + Y·Py = N·F (where P = partial derivative)

mnemonics.title: Homogeneity Check
mnemonics.content: To check homogeneous: Replace x→tx, y→ty, Simplify → If t^n comes out, that's your degree. Like pulling t out of a hat (homogeneous degree!)

mnemonics.title: Degree Zero Property
mnemonics.content: Zero Degree = Zero Result: For degree 0, X·Px + Y·Py = 0. Remember: Nothing from nothing leaves nothing!

mnemonics.title: Multi-variable Euler
mnemonics.content: Sum Everything: Σ (xᵢ · ∂f/∂xᵢ) = n · f. The Greek letter Sigma (Σ) reminds you to sum all variables!

mnemonics.title: Applications
mnemonics.content: TED: Thermodynamics, Economics, Derivatives optimization. Euler helps in all three ED areas!

acronyms.expansion: Euler's Useful Law Establishing Relationship - x·fx' + y·fy' = n·f(x,y)
acronyms.usage: Remembering the formula structure: E-uler's U-seful L-aw E-stablishing R-elationship between partials and function

acronyms.expansion: Homogeneous Of Making Output Generated by Exponents Nice
acronyms.usage: Checking if function is homogeneous: all terms must have same total exponent

acronyms.expansion: No Returns To Scale - when n≠1 (for production functions)
acronyms.usage: In economics: n<1 means decreasing returns, n>1 means increasing returns, n=1 means constant returns

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a rubber sheet being stretched equally in x and y directions. The function's value scales by t^n, like the area scaling by t² for 2D stretch.
Think of X and Y as weights on a balance scale, with partial derivatives as their 'rates'. The product sums equal n times the total function value.
Each term in the function should have the same number of 'balls' when you count variables. x²y has 3 balls (2 x-balls + 1 y-ball), xy² also has 3.
A degree 0 function is like a ratio - it stays the same when scaled. Like 3:5 ratio stays 3:5 whether you multiply by 2 or 10.

analogies.analogy: Like a recipe scaled proportionally - if you double all ingredients, the cake tastes the same, just bigger. Homogeneous functions maintain their 'recipe' when scaled.
Like a country's GDP being proportional to its population times productivity. The 'productivity' (function) scales with the 'population' (variables) by a fixed degree.
Think of a math expression like a team of players - all players must have equal 'strength' (exponent sum) for the team to be homogeneous.
Like a perfectly designed machine where all parts scale together - doubling all components doubles the total performance, following Euler's relationship.
